policy_statement: Qatar commissioned a CO2 recovery and sequestration facility at Ras Laffan in 2019 with a design capacity of 2.2 MTPA of CO2 capture and storage.
mechanism_description: Commissioning a CO2 recovery and sequestration facility captures CO2 from operations and stores it, reducing point-source CO2 emissions released to the atmosphere.
primary_causal_pathway: Industrial point-source CO2 capture → CO2 compression/transport → permanent storage (geologic sequestration) → ↓ atmospheric CO2 emissions → ↓ radiative forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Industrial facilities add CCS equipment and operations (capture unit, dehydration/compression, transport, injection wells, monitoring) to divert CO2 from flue gas or process streams.  
(2) Mediating physical process: CO2 is separated, compressed, and injected into secure storage (e.g., saline aquifers/depleted reservoirs) where it is trapped structurally/residually and/or mineralizes over time, preventing release.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower net point-source CO2 emissions to the atmosphere (accounting for capture rate and any energy-related “parasitic load”), yielding emissions mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: Capturing and permanently storing CO2 from an industrial point source avoids atmospheric CO2 emissions, reducing net greenhouse gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The central effect is physical removal/diversion of CO2 from an industrial exhaust or process stream into permanent storage, thereby reducing net atmospheric CO2 emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While CCS can have energy/resource implications (additional energy use, solvents, compression), those are secondary; the intended and largest climate-relevant effect of CCS deployment is emissions mitigation via avoided CO2 release. It is not primarily adaptation, resource efficiency, or nature-based.  
3) Edge cases applied: If CO2 is used for enhanced oil recovery (EOR) or short-lived utilization (U-CCU) that leads to re-release, the net mitigation benefit can be reduced or ambiguous; however, the canonical mechanism specified is sequestration leading to point-source CO2 reduction (i.e., storage), which remains mitigation.  
4) Confirm applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that commissions or enables CO2 capture plus sequestration from industrial operations shares this same dominant pathway of avoided atmospheric CO2 emissions, so “Mitigation” applies consistently.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer categorization (e.g., industrial decarbonization, hard-to-abate sector measure, compliance vs voluntary, capture type) depends on policy-specific design and evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential edge cases include CO2 use in EOR or non-permanent utilization, high upstream methane/energy emissions for capture and compression, low capture rates, or leakage/insufficient MRV; these affect net mitigation magnitude but not the primary category for sequestration-focused CCS.
key_indicators: “commissioned”; “CO2 recovery and sequestration facility”; “design capacity of 2.2 MTPA”; “CO2 capture and storage”; “Ras Laffan”
instance_edge_case_notes: While the text does not explicitly state the funding/ownership model (public vs private), “commissioned” and the emphasis on facility build-out supports classification as infrastructure investment rather than a regulatory or pricing instrument.
